what_it_does: This CSS file styles a 'Design System workspace (Decisions)' interface: a master-detail list of design decisions on desktop and a native drill-in view on mobile, plus visual specimens that preview token families such as radius, density, warmth, accent, motion, and voice.
observations: BEM-style class prefixes: .dsys for the workspace and .dspec for specimens
Master-detail grid (296px sidebar + fluid detail) on desktop, collapsing to a toggled single-pane drill-in on mobile via .dsys__grid--detail
Decision rows (.dsys__row) have hover, active, icon, title, current-value, and chevron/dot affordances
Option cards (.dsys__opt) are arranged in responsive grids (3, 5, 4, or 1 columns depending on category) with active selection rings and recommendation/activity flags
Each decision detail shows a question, rationale, affected tokens, and a 'play' action
Specimens include radius cards, density bars, warmth paper wash, accent button/chip/link, motion easing curve and runner, and voice label/bubble
Extensive use of CSS custom properties for colors, spacing, radii, typography, shadows, and transitions
Mobile breakpoint at 640px hides the summary and master pane and shows a back button
standards: Mobile drill-in pattern implemented purely through display toggling with a modifier class (.dsys__grid--detail)
Specimen sections mix token variables with intentional hardcoded preview colors (e.g., #FBF7EC, #988058, rgba(120,95,60,0.18))
Motion specimen includes an animated runner with an 850ms transform transition
conventions: Follows BEM naming with double underscores and double dashes for modifiers
Relies on a token registry via CSS custom properties (--bg-surface, --ms-hairline, --accent-gold, etc.)
Uses transition shorthand with var(--dur-base) and var(--ease-out)
Responsive layouts use CSS Grid and media queries; mobile simplifies grids to a single column
Pill-shaped chips and rounded cards are used consistently for labels, flags, and buttons
concerns: Some specimen colors are hardcoded hex/rgba values rather than tokens, which may drift from the token system
Mobile drill-in depends on JavaScript adding/removing .dsys__grid--detail; without JS the detail pane is hidden
No reduced-motion or prefers-reduced-motion handling for the animated motion specimen
No fallback values for CSS custom properties, so missing tokens will break layout/color
notes: A token-driven stylesheet for a design-decision browser with built-in preview specimens and a mobile drill-in toggle.
questions: What JavaScript adds or removes the .dsys__grid--detail class on mobile?
Where are the CSS custom properties/tokens (e.g., --accent-gold, --ms-hairline) defined?
Are the hardcoded specimen colors meant to be locally overridden per token family, or are they static reference swatches?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the file: it builds a master-detail decision workspace and uses token variables throughout. The minor mismatch is that some preview specimens use hardcoded colors despite the comment that values flow from token registries.
intention_signals: The file appears to be trying to give designers or developers a browsable, interactive catalog of design decisions where each decision explains its rationale, lists affected tokens, and renders a live visual specimen.
novelty: It combines decision rationale, token impact labels, and category-specific animated specimens into a single cohesive workspace component vocabulary.
contribution: It contributes a structured, responsive CSS vocabulary and specimen system for a design-decision UI, including master-detail layout, option cards, selection states, and token-preview specimens.
summary_for_embedding: CSS stylesheet for a design-system decision workspace with a master-detail layout, selectable option cards, token-impact metadata, and category-specific preview specimens for radius, density, warmth, accent, motion, and voice.
intention_for_embedding: To style an interactive design-decision browser that lets users inspect, compare, and select token-driven design choices with live visual previews.
